title: New Zealand Town

jokes.text: The audience count: 'How many?' — 'One.'
jokes.setup: Murray excitedly tells the band the audience is in and they can go on, implying a crowd is waiting.
jokes.punchline: The audience is exactly one person.
jokes.explanation: Efficient comedic reveal — the pause before 'one' lands cleanly. Murray's energetic opener sets an expectation of normalcy that is instantly demolished. Classic FOTC economy of language.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, it's not, Bret. Actually, Mel was busy. She actually requested that she can't come today. But if she was here, yes, we'd have two. But unfortunately we're down to one today.

jokes.text: 'Is it Mel?' — 'No, it's not, Bret. Actually, Mel was busy. She actually requested that she can't come today. But if she was here, yes, we'd have two.'
jokes.setup: They have one audience member. Bret hopefully asks if it's Mel, their only reliable fan.
jokes.punchline: Even Mel — their most devoted (stalker-ish) fan — deliberately opted out, meaning the one stranger in the audience is their best result.
jokes.explanation: The joke is layered: Bret's hope that their only audience member is their obsessive fan reveals how desperate their situation is. Murray's qualifier 'if she was here, yes, we'd have two' is perfectly deadpan math comedy. Strong character integration because Mel's superfan status is essential to the joke's meaning.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a big guy though, Jemaine. He's taking up three seats. He's got shopping bags with him.

jokes.text: 'He's a big guy though, Jemaine. He's taking up three seats. He's got shopping bags with him.' — 'Well, you can't count the shopping bags, can you?' — 'There's three seats used.' — 'What, so the majority of the audience is shopping bags?' — 'Yeah, most of the audience are shopping bags.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to spin the single audience member as occupying three seats due to his size and bags.
jokes.punchline: The logical conclusion is accepted without resistance: the majority of the audience is shopping bags, and Murray earnestly defends this framing.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The escalation from one audience member → three seats → majority of audience are shopping bags is perfectly structured absurdist logic. Murray's commitment to the positive spin ('look at it that there's three seats taken') is character-specific — only Murray would earnestly reframe inanimate objects as audience members. Quotable and deeply silly in a way that rewards the show's deadpan register.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, so the majority of the audience is shopping bags?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, most of the audience are shopping bags.

jokes.text: 'Just give this one big guy with the shopping bags the best gig of his life. Rock it out.'
jokes.setup: They've established the 'audience' is mostly shopping bags.
jokes.punchline: Murray delivers an earnest motivational send-off as if they're about to perform to a packed arena.
jokes.explanation: Murray's earnest sincerity in impossible circumstances is a running character note. The line lands because of the accumulated absurdity — he's really committing to the bit. 'Rock it out' as the pep talk for a one-man-shopping-bag audience is a satisfying button.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, when did he go? When did that guy leave?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, he... he snuck out. Possibly as early as the first song.

jokes.text: Jemaine's reaction beat: 'One person. This is a new low.' — delivered flatly to camera (implied talking head).
jokes.setup: The elaborate spin Murray put on the audience situation.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine cuts through all of it with two sentences of absolute deadpan reality.
jokes.explanation: 'This is a new low' becomes a running gag across the episode. First deployment here is efficient and perfectly timed — the phrase does heavy lifting for very few words. Jemaine's flatness is essential.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Technically I actually snuck out as well.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You snuck out? What do you mean you snuck out?

jokes.text: Post-gig: 'Hey, when did he go? When did that guy leave?' — 'Yeah, he snuck out. Possibly as early as the first song.'
jokes.setup: They thank the one audience member after presumably performing a full set.
jokes.punchline: The single audience member left, possibly during the very first song — meaning they performed the entire gig to literally no one.
jokes.explanation: 'Possibly as early as the first song' is the killer qualifier — Murray's uncertainty about when the desertion happened makes it worse and funnier. The escalation from one person to zero is a perfectly structured disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, you went shopping.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah.

jokes.text: The audience member (and manager) both snuck out during the gig.
jokes.setup: The guys performed for one audience member. They thank the audience member, then realize he's gone.
jokes.punchline: Murray reveals he also snuck out during the gig, immediately followed by his defensive deflection to the other guy.
jokes.explanation: Two-stage escalation works perfectly: first the audience snuck out (bad), then the MANAGER snuck out (worse). Murray's immediate pivot — 'What about the other guy? He snuck out. He's not our manager though.' — is classic sitcom structure executed with this show's specific deadpan flavor. His acknowledgment that yes, the distinction matters, makes it land harder.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's when I didn't have to pay the meter. Remember the meter was broken when I parked outside your apartment? That's the high point of our career?

jokes.text: 'Technically yes.' / 'Well, technically you were there.' / 'Technically I actually snuck out as well.'
jokes.setup: They ask if they played to an empty room.
jokes.punchline: Murray, their manager, also snuck out during the gig — leaving them literally unattended by anyone.
jokes.explanation: The triple-step escalation — audience gone, technically you were there, technically I snuck out too — is beautifully structured. Murray preemptively defending himself ('I knew you were gonna get angry. Don't single me out') before being confronted is specific Murray behavior. The reveal that he went shopping is a perfect cherry. One of the stronger beats in the cold open.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I still think about it now. It was such a great night.

jokes.text: 'You went shopping.' — 'Oh, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to deflect blame for abandoning his band's gig by noting the audience also left.
jokes.punchline: He went shopping. He simply went shopping. He concedes this with zero shame.
jokes.explanation: The brevity and Murray's total lack of remorse makes this land. 'Oh, yeah' is the ideal two-word confession.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's not the '90s. Okay, my point is the difference between cool bands and you is hair.

jokes.text: Murray produces a 'high points and low points graph' — the high point of their entire career is a parking meter that was broken.
jokes.setup: Murray announces they've hit a new low point and pulls out a career graph for context.
jokes.punchline: The highest point on the graph — their career apex — is a time Murray didn't have to pay a parking meter.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. The career graph is already a funny prop, but the specific content of the high point — a free parking meter — is inspired. It's the kind of mundane personal joy that Murray would genuinely rank above any musical achievement. 'I still think about it now. It was such a great night.' delivered with sincerity is what elevates this above a simple low-expectations gag. Strong Murray characterization, highly quotable logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I've got hair. True.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But most of your hair is body hair, isn't it, Jemaine?

jokes.text: 'Maybe in the '70s, guys. But come on, get up with the '90s.' — 'It's not the '90s.'
jokes.setup: Bret argues it's about the music, not the look.
jokes.punchline: Murray says to 'get up with the '90s' — but it is not the '90s.
jokes.explanation: Murray's temporal confusion (confidently referencing the '90s as aspirational currency in what is clearly the 2000s) is consistent with his character as someone perpetually slightly behind. Bret's flat correction is the ideal response. Compact and effective.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: A moss-green shirt and a tie? Obviously this is for work. Up here is for play, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, this is it.

jokes.text: 'Most of your hair is body hair, isn't it, Jemaine?'
jokes.setup: Murray argues the difference between cool bands and Bret and Jemaine is hair.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine technically has hair, but most of it is body hair — undermining the entire concession.
jokes.explanation: Well-structured reversal — granting the premise ('True') before immediately collapsing it. The specificity of 'body hair' makes it funnier than a generic insult. Jemaine's simple 'Well, some of it' is a perfectly resigned confirmation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Morning, prime minister.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Thank you, m'lord.

jokes.text: Murray's 1991 'experiments' photos — he was 'trying to find my look.' Bret: 'You should dress like this more often.' Murray: 'No, I've found my look now.' His look is a moss-green shirt and a tie.
jokes.setup: Murray presents old photos of himself experimenting with fashion in 1991 while advocating for style.
jokes.punchline: His 'winning look' he settled on is a deeply unremarkable moss-green shirt and tie, presented with complete confidence. The old experimental looks were presumably wilder and better.
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy here (experimental Murray photos from 1991 vs. his settled bland look) works on character levels — Murray genuinely believes in his winning look. 'Obviously this is for work. Up here is for play' — implying the gel is 'play Murray' — is a lovely additional layer.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: We're gonna have Gary the sheep. He's flying over from New Zealand. Really? Yes, New Zealand's most famous sheep is gonna be there. He's on hiatus.

jokes.text: The Prime Minister interrupts with 'Knock knock' and walks in. Murray immediately says 'Morning, prime minister.' Jemaine: 'Thank you, m'lord.'
jokes.setup: The Prime Minister of New Zealand casually drops into the New Zealand consulate office.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine addresses the Prime Minister as 'm'lord' — an entirely wrong honorific delivered with apparent sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's 'm'lord' lands as a quiet bit of character comedy — he's not sure of the right honorific and goes medieval. Murray's immediate, practiced 'Morning, prime minister' vs. Jemaine's fumble is a nice contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: New Zealandtown between Chinatown and Little Italy - which isn't exactly accurate geographically

jokes.text: Prime Minister asks Murray for an eraser because 'I'm doing a drawing but I've made a mistake. I'm working on a poster idea for my new campaign.'
jokes.setup: The Prime Minister of New Zealand is on an official state visit to the consulate.
jokes.punchline: He's primarily concerned about getting an eraser for what appears to be a hand-drawn campaign poster.
jokes.explanation: Establishes the PM as a lovably small-stakes figure. The hand-drawn poster with a mistake needing an eraser signals that New Zealand's government operates on a charmingly modest level. Consistent with the show's running joke about New Zealand's relative smallness.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: We have a chap shearing him. Well, pretending to shear him. Be mime shearing him. Mime shearing him because Gary's got his look. We can't mess with that.

jokes.text: New Zealandtown will be 'one block between Chinatown and little Italy, which isn't exactly accurate geographically, but it's perfect for our needs.'
jokes.setup: The PM describes the plan to establish New Zealandtown in NYC like Chinatown or Little Italy.
jokes.punchline: It's not geographically accurate (New Zealand is nowhere near China or Italy) but they're doing it anyway.
jokes.explanation: The PM's breezy dismissal of geographical accuracy — 'it's perfect for our needs' — is solid absurdist logic. The show's gentle mockery of New Zealand's self-importance is consistent here.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Would you like us to play?

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Well, no, we're looking for a cool band.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They are available.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We're considered quite cool, aren't we?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: In their minds, yes.

jokes.text: Gary the sheep: 'New Zealand's most famous sheep is gonna be there. He's on hiatus.' A person will 'mime shearing him because Gary's got his look. We can't mess with that.'
jokes.setup: The PM describes the celebrity attractions of New Zealandtown.
jokes.punchline: The headline attraction is a sheep — who is a celebrity with an established look that cannot be altered, requiring mime shearing to protect the look.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist comedy specific to New Zealand's cultural identity. Gary the sheep as a celebrity 'on hiatus' with a signature look that must be protected is inspired. The detail that he needs mime-shearing to preserve his look treats the sheep with the same PR caution applied to a human celebrity. Also sets up the devastating callback when Gary gets accidentally shorn for real.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ew!

jokes.text: PM asks for a cool band. Bret and Jemaine immediately offer. PM: 'Well, no, we're looking for a cool band.' Murray: 'They are available.' PM: 'I see.'
jokes.setup: PM mentions needing a cool band for the event, clearly speaking generally.
jokes.punchline: Bret and Jemaine immediately volunteer; the PM's polite but clear 'Well, no, we're looking for a cool band' is a devastating dismissal, softened only by Murray's unhelpful 'they are available.'
jokes.explanation: The beat works because the PM doesn't even have to say 'not you' — 'we're looking for a cool band' does the work while technically not being an insult. Murray offering their availability as consolation is characteristically oblivious. 'I see' from the PM is a perfect note of diplomatic awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How's that?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That looks cool.

jokes.text: 'If you really want to help, we do need someone to work the information booth.' — Bret and Jemaine are given the information booth job. 'You guys keen?' — 'No.' — 'Great.'
jokes.setup: After being passed over for the band slot, the PM offers them a consolation job.
jokes.punchline: They say 'No' and the PM accepts this and immediately confirms 'Great' as if they agreed.
jokes.explanation: 'No.' — 'Great.' is structurally perfect two-word comedy. The PM either doesn't hear or doesn't care about the refusal, and presses forward. A tiny joke with a very clean structure.

dialogue.line: Fashion song sequence

jokes.text: Reaction to the hair gel — implied visual sequence of people reacting to Bret and Jemaine's gelled hair. 'Don't touch it.' then 'Ew!'
jokes.setup: Murray has given them styling gel and they've done their hair. A series of reactions from strangers play out.
jokes.punchline: Implied physical comedy of over-gelled hair — 'Don't touch it' and 'Ew!' suggest the gel has been applied in an extreme, off-putting quantity.
jokes.explanation: Inferred visual gag — the transcript only gives us 'Don't touch it' and 'Ew!' but these clearly function as reaction beats to an absurd hair appearance. The payoff depends heavily on the visual, which works on screen but reads lightly in transcript.

dialogue.line: You think fashion's your friend, my friend, fashion is danger

jokes.text: Escalating 'cool' reactions from strangers: 'How's that?' / 'That looks cool.' / 'Whoa, that is cool.' / 'You look like a punk rocker.' / 'Very cool look, man.' / 'Totally cool.' / 'Pretty cool.' / 'Cool.'
jokes.setup: After applying gel, Bret and Jemaine check their appearance and solicit feedback.
jokes.punchline: A montage of increasingly enthusiastic 'cool' affirmations from multiple people creates a comedic swell — the gel is genuinely working.
jokes.explanation: Functions as comic escalation that sets up the fashion song. The rhythm of affirmations builds nicely. The joke is structural rather than linguistic — the comedy is in the accumulation and the irony that styling gel could actually transform their social reception.

dialogue.line: Posing sequence - posing at the bar, posing sitting down, posing in the distance, posing with my arm, posing with my leg, posing like a swan, posing for a portrait, posing a threat

jokes.text: THE FASHION SONG — 'Fashion / f-f-f-f-fashion, f-fashion / s-s-s-style, the l-l-l-l-look' — opening stutter-rap delivery of a fashion anthem.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine, having been told they need style, perform a song about fashion.
jokes.punchline: The song opens with a David Bowie-esque 'Fashion' parody but with stammered, fragmented delivery that immediately undercuts any genuine cool.
jokes.explanation: The stutter-rap delivery is a parody of both Bowie's 'Fashion' and hip-hop affectation — two genres the Conchords have no business attempting, which is the joke. The visual of two awkward New Zealanders doing this would amplify it considerably.

jokes.text: ¶ you think you know fashion / well, fashion's a stranger / you think fashion's your friend / my friend, fashion is danger ¶
jokes.setup: Fashion song hits a philosophical chorus warning about the treachery of fashion.
jokes.punchline: Fashion being a 'stranger' and 'danger' is both a rhyme scheme and a genuinely absurd threat level to assign to fashion trends. The deadly seriousness of the warning is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The best lyric in the fashion song. 'Fashion is danger' is delivered with the gravity of a PSA about drug addiction. The stranger/danger rhyme is deliberately cheesy while the underlying logic is weirdly compelling — and of course the episode proves the point, as the hair gel does become genuinely dangerous. Quotable and thematically resonant.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's Bret and Jemaine. You know, my band. They're bloody great.

jokes.text: Prime Minister sees Bret and Jemaine: 'You guys seem a little cooler than usual today.' — 'Usually you wear clothes from the '70s.' — 'They're not from the '70s. They're from new zealand.' — 'Isn't that the same thing?' — 'Similar, I suppose.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are described as wearing clothes from the '70s.
jokes.punchline: Murray's correction that the clothes are from New Zealand — not the '70s — is immediately accepted as equivalent by the PM, and Murray doesn't disagree.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest exchanges. 'They're not from the '70s, they're from New Zealand' as a defense, followed by 'Isn't that the same thing?' / 'Similar, I suppose' — Murray conceding the point is the kicker. It's a three-beat national self-deprecation joke that's very precise: the PM of New Zealand agrees New Zealand fashion is equivalent to 1970s fashion, and Murray accepts this. Highly quotable, character-integrated, and delivered with ideal economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They're not from the '70s. They're from New Zealand.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Isn't that the same thing?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Similar, I suppose.

jokes.text: The 'glowing review' from the New Zealand Consulate Newsletter: 'They came, they saw, they Conchord. A couple of cool guys took to the stage and wowed the audience with music and up-to-the-minute styles. Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool.'
jokes.setup: Murray announces a glowing review from the night's gig.
jokes.punchline: The review is from the New Zealand Consulate Newsletter — written, presumably, by Murray himself — and contains the pun 'they Conchord' plus 'Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool' as the closing line.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke: the source (a newsletter Murray controls), the pun ('they Conchord'), and the closing line ('Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool') as Murray's idea of a killer sign-off. The refrigerator line especially — it's trying to be clever and landing as wonderfully strange. High character integration because this is exactly how Murray would write about his own band.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Whatever, Murray. Hey, great gig last night. You got a glowing review.

jokes.text: 'Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool.'
jokes.setup: Murray is reading the glowing newsletter review aloud.
jokes.punchline: The review's closing line is a refrigerator pun for 'cool' — absurdly lowbrow wordplay from what is ostensibly journalism, delivered by Murray with the same pride as if it were a New York Times review.
jokes.explanation: The refrigerator pun is aggressively bad — the joke is that this passes for wit in the NZ Consulate Newsletter, and Murray is proud of it. Meta-humor about terrible puns presented as serious praise.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: New Zealand Consulate Newsletter... 'They came, they saw, they conchord. A couple of cool guys took to the stage and wowed the audience with music and up-to-the-minute styles. Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool.'

jokes.text: 'Whatever, Murray.' / 'Whatever, David.' — Bret and Jemaine's new 'cool guy' persona manifests as dismissing everyone including their friend David.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have adopted cool-guy attitudes with their new hair.
jokes.punchline: They dismiss everyone including a departing friend ('Whatever, David') with the same aloof contempt, even though there's no context for who David is or why he deserves it.
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag. The 'whatever' affectation is amusing in Bret and Jemaine's mouths because it's so clearly performed. 'Whatever, David' to a presumably ordinary departing friend who gets no further characterization is a nice absurdist beat.

dialogue.line: Complex chain of command explanation ending with discretionary power over Greg

jokes.text: 'Go away, Murray.' — Murray: 'Go away!' — '...Did you hear that, prime minister? So cool.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine dismisses Murray as part of his new cool-guy persona.
jokes.punchline: Murray is so delighted by being told to go away that he repeats it to the PM as evidence of coolness, attributing the insult as a credential.
jokes.explanation: Murray's enthusiasm for being dismissed — sharing it with the PM like a proud parent — is a very specific character beat. He's marketing the band's rudeness as a feature. The PM's implied bewilderment plays against Murray's earnestness.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Paula grew up next to me. 39 Harcourt Street, Nelson. Mind you, that was all a very very long time ago. Many many years ago now. A lot's happened since then. Things change. People get married to other people.

jokes.text: Murray and Paula's awkward implied history — 'Paula grew up next to me. 39 Harcourt Street, Nelson. Mind you, that was all a very very long time ago. Many many years ago now. People get married to other people.' — both of them talking past each other.
jokes.setup: Murray introduces Paula and the PM casually mentions their shared past.
jokes.punchline: Murray's strained specificity (the address, the years, 'people get married to OTHER people') reveals a complicated history. Neither Murray nor Paula will address it directly.
jokes.explanation: FOTC excels at submerged emotional comedy — the history between Murray and Paula is entirely delivered through evasion and over-specificity. The address '39 Harcourt Street, Nelson' is a funny detail that suggests Murray still remembers it vividly. 'People get married to other people' as a philosophical observation is the standout line.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Paula's second in command after me and then you and then Greg. So if I wanted to, I could tell her to do something and then she'd tell you to do it. But I could tell Greg. That's at your discretion.

jokes.text: The PM tests his new chain of command: 'Paula, could you please ask Murray what time it is?' — Paula: 'Hey, Murray, have you got the time?' — Murray: '2:00.' — Paula: 'It's 2:00, prime minister.' — PM: 'Yes, I heard and I think he heard me.'
jokes.setup: The PM has established a new hierarchical system and wants to test it.
jokes.punchline: The system is tested by asking for the time — an absurdly mundane request for an elaborate chain of command — and the PM acknowledges that everyone in the room heard the time being stated, making the chain pointless.
jokes.explanation: Excellent bureaucratic absurdism. The chain-of-command test uses the most trivial possible request (the time) to satirize hierarchical thinking. The PM's observation 'Yes, I heard and I think he heard me' — acknowledging the entire system is unnecessary — is the perfect button. 'On a basic level this works, but I think we could use more development' is a wonderful straight-faced assessment of a useless system. One of the episode's best non-musical jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: On a basic level this works, but I think we could use more development.

jokes.text: Maori village plan: 'I think the most important part is to get a maori warrior. That would really sell it. So do we know any maoris, Jemaine?' — 'Actually, you're part maori, aren't you?' — 'No, I'm not doing it.'
jokes.setup: Murray wants an authentic Maori warrior for the New Zealandtown attraction.
jokes.punchline: The casting solution is immediately Jemaine (who is part Maori) — and Jemaine's instant 'No, I'm not doing it' before even being formally asked suggests he's dealt with this before.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on the cringe comedy of ethnic tokenism being deployed casually by Murray. Jemaine's preemptive refusal is the best beat — he doesn't wait to be asked because he already knows what's coming. Murray's earnest 'Please?' makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Paula, could you please ask Murray what time it is?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Um, hey, Murray, have you got the time?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: It's 2:00, prime minister.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Yes, I heard and I think he heard me.

jokes.text: 'Well, if you don't do it we're gonna have to get Mexicans.' — 'You can't get Mexicans to do it. That's culturally insensitive.' — 'We've already signed one up so it can't be too sensitive.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine refuses to play the Maori warrior.
jokes.punchline: Murray's threat to use Mexicans as substitute Maori warriors — and having already pre-booked one — is absurdly insensitive, with the logic that pre-booking makes it acceptable.
jokes.explanation: The kicker 'we've already signed one up so it can't be too sensitive' is a superb piece of bureaucratic logic — if you've already completed the paperwork, the ethical concern evaporates. This is Murray's character crystallized: well-meaning, utterly obtuse, extremely organized about the wrong things. Quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Actually, you're part Maori, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But you are part Maori?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, but I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please be the Maori.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Hair gel fight — Bret and Jemaine accuse each other of using the gel on body hair and beard, then throw the tube at each other. 'Oh! That was very dangerous.' / 'How dare you throw this at me?' / 'I think it's a fitting revenge for you throwing it at me.' / 'Oh! That was dangerous.'
jokes.setup: The gel is running out and they're both trying to blame the other for excessive use.
jokes.punchline: The argument escalates into a tube-throwing fight, with both treating the projectile as genuinely life-threatening ('very dangerous,' 'That was dangerous') despite it being a tube of hair gel.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works better on screen than in transcript. The mock-gravity treatment of a hair gel tube as a dangerous weapon is consistent with FOTC's gentle absurdism. 'I think it's a fitting revenge for you throwing it at me' as the most formal way to justify throwing something back is a nice touch. Functional but not standout.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, if you don't do it we're gonna have to get Mexicans. You can't get Mexicans to do it. That's culturally insensitive. We've already signed one up so it can't be too sensitive.

jokes.text: 'Did you finish the gel on your body hair?' / 'N...no.' / 'You've been gelling your beard.'
jokes.setup: They've run out of gel. Bret interrogates Jemaine about where it went.
jokes.punchline: Callback to the body hair joke — Jemaine has apparently used the shared gel supply on his beard (and possibly other body hair), depleting it.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — the earlier joke about Jemaine's body hair is now plot-relevant. The hesitation before 'N...no' and the damning 'your beard looks good' confirmation are perfectly timed. Callbacks that serve plot function are the best kind.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, where's the gel gone? What happened to all the gel?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You know what happened to the gel.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You used it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Did you finish the gel on your body hair?

jokes.text: Toothbrush fence replica: 'Paula, how many toothbrushes have we got so far for the replica of the toothbrush fence? 46.' — 'Yeah, well, that's still not 50 and we need 50 really for an effective replica.'
jokes.setup: The PM is overseeing preparations for New Zealandtown.
jokes.punchline: New Zealand apparently has a famous toothbrush fence, and the PM is deeply invested in getting the replica count exactly right.
jokes.explanation: A very New Zealand joke — the toothbrush fence is a real New Zealand landmark (Blenheim area), and the PM's precision about needing exactly 50 for an 'effective replica' is sweetly absurd. The chain-of-command dispatch ('Can you talk to Murray about organizing some more?' → Murray → Greg → 'I'll bring one in') is bureaucratic comedy in miniature.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You've been gelling your beard. Your beard looks good.

jokes.text: 'Can I drive the bus?' — 'Don't think that's a very good idea.' — 'The prime minister of New Zealand driving a bus?' — 'Please?' — 'Please, Murray?' — 'All right then.' — 'Excellent. I get to drive the tour bus.'
jokes.setup: Murray has booked a tour bus for New Zealandtown visitors.
jokes.punchline: The Prime Minister really wants to personally drive the tour bus, is denied for dignity reasons, asks 'please' twice, and is granted permission — celebrating this as a personal victory.
jokes.explanation: The PM's childlike glee at being allowed to drive a bus — and the 'please?' capitulation dynamic — is perfect small-stakes comedy. This PM is delightfully uninterested in being dignified. 'I get to drive the tour bus' delivered as triumph is an ideal character moment.

dialogue.line: Gel throwing fight sequence with 'That was very dangerous' reactions

jokes.text: 'Did you ever think I'd end up being prime minister, Paula?' — 'No. Complete surprise to me.'
jokes.setup: The PM asks Paula — his old neighbor — a rhetorical question implying he's exceeded expectations.
jokes.punchline: Paula gives the most honest possible answer: 'No. Complete surprise to me.' — with no softening.
jokes.explanation: Economy of brutality. Paula's zero-hesitation 'No. Complete surprise to me.' is the correct response to a bad rhetorical question and the scene's timing makes it land perfectly. Five words and a period do more work than a paragraph.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Well, Gary, you must be jet-lagged. Let's get you off to your accommodations.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Did you ever think I'd end up being prime minister, Paula?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: No. Complete surprise to me.

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine are too afraid to go outside without gel: 'We'll wait till there's no one out there.' — They wait and wait.
jokes.setup: They've run out of gel and can't risk being seen without it.
jokes.punchline: They agree to wait until there's no one outside — presumably forever, since they're in NYC.
jokes.explanation: Gentle physical comedy of two men held hostage by hair product. The implied visual of them crouching/hiding while the city goes about its business is funnier on screen. Works as character comedy of extreme vanity from previously extremely un-vain people.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's people. They'll see us.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No one can see me like this.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You go.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I can't go out there. Not without gel.

jokes.text: Murray opens the door, sees their hair without gel: 'Oh my God! Oh, it's terrible!' — 'What have you done?'
jokes.setup: Murray comes to check on them after hearing they need gel.
jokes.punchline: Murray's reaction to their gel-free hair is one of absolute horror, as if he's witnessed something catastrophic.
jokes.explanation: Works as visual reaction comedy. Murray's horror depends entirely on what we see, which in the transcript we can only infer. 'What have you done?' as if they've committed a crime is a nice over-reaction. Stronger on screen.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Oh, it's terrible. What have you done?

jokes.text: 'There's still some gel.' — 'You're imagining the gel.' — 'There's still some.' — 'There's no gel left. It's not there.'
jokes.setup: They're desperately scraping an empty gel tube.
jokes.punchline: One of them is hallucinating gel in an empty tube — the other has to reality-check him.
jokes.explanation: Desperation comedy — treating hair gel with the urgency of a vital resource running out. 'You're imagining the gel' is a funny line. Plays as escalation of their gel-dependency subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's still some gel. There's still a little bit of gel.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You're imagining the gel.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's still some.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's no gel left. It's not there.

jokes.text: 'What if he's keeping the gel for himself to make himself look good?' — Beat — 'Don't think so, man. That big ginger head of his...'
jokes.setup: Murray is taking a long time bringing the gel.
jokes.punchline: The implication that Murray could look good with gel is undercut by Jemaine's observation about Murray's 'big ginger head' — delivered as a reason gel wouldn't help him anyway.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's matter-of-fact dismissal of Murray's gel-appropriation conspiracy based on Murray's appearance is a light character beat. 'That big ginger head of his' is affectionately devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What if he's keeping the gel for himself to make himself look good?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Don't think so, man. That big ginger head of his looking great.

jokes.text: The PM starts drinking beer on the tour bus and has to be reminded he can't drink in public. 'Well, we're in new zealandtown now. There's nothing they can do about it.'
jokes.setup: The PM is at the wheel of the tour bus for the New Zealandtown opening ceremony.
jokes.punchline: He attempts to invoke extraterritorial diplomatic immunity for New Zealandtown, arguing that within one NYC block he is sovereign and New Zealand drinking laws (or lack thereof) apply.
jokes.explanation: The PM's casual sovereignty claim over one block of Manhattan is consistent with the show's portrait of New Zealand's cheerful self-delusion. 'There's nothing they can do about it' from a man drinking while driving a bus is perfectly delivered. The follow-up that he also can't drink and drive — accepted cheerfully — is a nice additional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: You can't drink in public over here.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Well, we're in New Zealandtown now. There's nothing they can do about it.

jokes.text: 'Oh my goodness me. Look at that. Quiet, everyone. It's some genuine native Māori warriors. We don't want to disturb them in their natural habitat.'
jokes.setup: The tour bus passes the Māori display that Murray has set up.
jokes.punchline: The PM performs the role of wildlife documentarian, talking about the Māori display as if they're discovering animals in nature — 'natural habitat,' 'don't disturb them' — despite this being a staged attraction on a Manhattan block.
jokes.explanation: The PM's narration treating a street exhibit as a wildlife encounter is well-executed. The colonial/anthropological register he adopts — 'natural habitat,' 'genuine native' — is pointed satire delivered with the show's trademark deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Quiet, everyone. It's some genuine native Maori warriors. We don't want to disturb them in their natural habitat. If you'd like to take pictures, do it very very quietly.

jokes.text: 'Okay, everybody off. That was new zealandtown.' — The tour ends after approximately 30 seconds.
jokes.setup: The tour bus has been driving through New Zealandtown.
jokes.punchline: The entire tour of New Zealandtown takes approximately 30 seconds — consistent with it being one block — and is announced as complete.
jokes.explanation: The brevity IS the joke. The idea of replicating Chinatown's scope in one block, and the tour lasting half a minute, lands as pure absurdist understatement. 'That was New Zealandtown' as a closing announcement is a great button.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Okay, everybody off. That was New Zealandtown.

jokes.text: Mel is discovered in their apartment, apparently sleepwalking or claiming to sleepwalk. 'This is your place. I must be sleepwalking. Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine arrive home to find Mel already there, feeding animals.
jokes.punchline: Mel immediately deploys 'sleepwalking' as her excuse for being in their apartment and uses the false belief that waking sleepwalkers is dangerous as a way to avoid further confrontation.
jokes.explanation: Mel's stalker behavior getting the sleepwalking defense is character-perfect — she has absolutely no credible alibi and immediately deploys an obviously false one. 'You could kill me' as a threat to discourage them from pursuing the matter is the kind of absurd defense only Mel would use. High character integration because this only works with Mel's established obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This isn't my place, is it? This is your place. I must be sleepwalking. Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.

jokes.text: 'Do you have a key to our apartment?' — Mel: 'This isn't my place, is it? This is your place.'
jokes.setup: They confront Mel about being in the apartment.
jokes.punchline: Mel deflects the key question by pretending to only just realize where she is — as if the confusion itself is the explanation.
jokes.explanation: Mel's non-answer to the key question is perfect evasion comedy. She never answers whether she has a key — which implies she does — while manufacturing enough confusion to avoid the direct question. Classic Mel misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Bird watching. I love to watch birds.

jokes.text: Mel's sleepwalking excuse: 'I must be sleepwalking. Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine find Mel in their apartment and confront her. They ask if she has a key.
jokes.punchline: Mel claims she's sleepwalking — an obvious lie — and then immediately deploys the myth that waking a sleepwalker is fatal as a way to avoid being confronted or removed.
jokes.explanation: The sleepwalking gambit is inspired — the lie is transparent, but Mel commits to it with total conviction and weaponizes the folk myth as a 'don't touch me' shield. 'Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.' has the rhythm and timing of a great joke. Strong Mel characterization — her stalker logic is consistently creative. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This is my sock.

jokes.text: Mel offers mousse or hairspray instead of gel, then turns out to have neither. 'I'd better go before I wake up.' — Bret: 'You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you?'
jokes.setup: Mel offers to help with their gel problem from her sock-drawer or pockets.
jokes.punchline: She has nothing. Her offer was completely hollow. She leaves maintaining the sleepwalking fiction while Bret delivers a genuinely angry 'What's wrong with you?' — treating 'no mousse' as a serious moral failing.
jokes.explanation: The triple-layer joke: Mel breaks into their apartment, offers hair products she doesn't have, finds a sock instead, and maintains the sleepwalking cover while being furiously told off for the empty promise of mousse. 'I don't know who you are anymore' from Jemaine over the mousse thing is the kicker — treating mousse availability as a character test.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.

jokes.text: 'Bird watching. I love to watch birds.' — Mel's non-sequitur while Jemaine searches her pockets.
jokes.setup: Mel claims to be sleepwalking while they search her for hair products.
jokes.punchline: As they rummage through her pockets, Mel maintains her sleepwalking performance by spontaneously narrating what she's 'dreaming' — 'bird watching, I love to watch birds' — as bizarre dream content.
jokes.explanation: Mel's commitment to the bit by ad-libbing dream content is excellent. 'Bird watching. I love to watch birds.' is the most innocent possible thing to 'dream' about — the specificity and mundanity of the lie-within-a-lie is what elevates it. She's not even dreaming something dramatic; her dream is birdwatching. Great deadpan absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.

jokes.text: 'I don't know who you are anymore.' — Said to Mel, about the mousse.
jokes.setup: Mel has failed to produce the promised hair mousse.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine delivers a profound statement of betrayal and identity loss — over hair product.
jokes.explanation: The best line of the Mel sequence. Treating the absence of mousse as an existential estrangement — a betrayal of their fundamental understanding of each other — is perfectly calibrated FOTC understatement. Jemaine's deadpan delivery makes it land as both genuinely devastating and completely absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Glue?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: This should work. Yeah yeah, this is good. It's just like gel. Same viscosity.

jokes.text: Mel produces glue from 'in here' (from her body, presumably a pocket or somewhere more alarming). Bret: 'Glue?' — Jemaine: 'This should work.' — 'It's just like gel. Same viscosity.'
jokes.setup: With no gel or mousse available, they need an alternative.
jokes.punchline: Mel produces glue from her person. Jemaine evaluates it scientifically ('same viscosity') and they proceed to use it.
jokes.explanation: The glue as gel substitute is perfectly escalating absurdism with an inevitable terrible payoff. 'Same viscosity' as Jemaine's scientific justification is the best detail — he's treating this as a rational decision. The joke seeds its own callback when the glue causes problems at the ceremony. One of the episode's stronger absurdist moments.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine, I stuck my head to my hand.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You idiot, Bret. You've got a pillow on your head.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What? Where? Other side.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, we're both idiots.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Idiot, man.

jokes.text: New Zealandtown opening speech: NZ 'were the first country in the world to research and develop the concept of a spreadable butter that can be used straight from the refrigerator.'
jokes.setup: The PM delivers an opening speech about New Zealand's spirit of innovation.
jokes.punchline: The historic innovation New Zealand is most proud of — their greatest contribution to civilization — is spreadable refrigerator butter.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single funniest joke and one of the best in the series. The PM delivers spreadable refrigerator butter as a world-historical achievement with complete sincerity and pride. The setup ('land of innovators... in that great spirit of innovation') primes you for something genuinely impressive, and the delivery of 'spreadable butter that can be used straight from the refrigerator' as the apex of human achievement is both very specific, very New Zealand, and absolutely absurd. Highly quotable, perfectly structured, and character-integrated. This is what a 9 looks like for this show.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi, Greg, I need some anti-glue.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: What's anti-glue, Murray?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, it's anti-glue, isn't it? It gets rid of glue.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I don't think that exists, Murray.

jokes.text: 'Like Chinatown or little ethiopia, and also similar to little Italy.'
jokes.setup: The PM lists ethnic neighborhoods as comparisons to New Zealandtown.
jokes.punchline: Little Ethiopia appears between Chinatown and Little Italy — a less prominent cultural enclave invoked with the same confidence, and 'and also similar to Little Italy' added as a separate clause suggests the PM is working from an incomplete list and patching it.
jokes.explanation: Minor callback joke (Little Italy was mentioned in the setup of New Zealandtown). The inclusion of Little Ethiopia as an equal comparator is a quiet odd choice that the PM treats as natural. Works as a background absurdist beat during a bigger sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's been shorn off. The guy who was supposed to mime shear him sheared him for real. He's basically bald. Yes. So there's your star, timid and nervous.

jokes.text: 'Jemaine, I stuck my head to my hand.' — 'You idiot, Bret.' — 'You've got a pillow on your head.' — 'What? Where?' — 'Other side.' — 'Oh, we're both idiots.'
jokes.setup: They used glue instead of gel.
jokes.punchline: Bret has glued his hand to his head; Jemaine has a pillow glued to his head. Both of them are idiots by their own admission.
jokes.explanation: The glue callback payoff. Hand glued to head and pillow glued to head are both excellent, but 'Oh, we're both idiots' as the shared conclusion is the perfect button. The escalating reveal — first the hand, then the pillow — is well-structured. Jemaine confirming 'other side' (directing Bret to find his pillow) before his own confession is a nice comic beat. High impact because the visual is inherently funny and the dialogue delivers perfectly.

dialogue.line: And to make matters worse, the guy next to him barbecuing... I think he's barbecuing lamb. Gary can tell. He's livid.

jokes.text: 'Shivers, I forgot your gel.' — 'Shivers' as Murray's expletive.
jokes.setup: Murray appears at the ceremony and Bret and Jemaine confront him about the gel.
jokes.punchline: Murray's expletive of choice is 'Shivers' — a deeply mild New Zealand exclamation deployed in a moment of genuine crisis.
jokes.explanation: One word does the job. 'Shivers' as Murray's strongest possible oath in a moment of genuine negligence is extremely character-specific New Zealand mild-mannered comedy. Murray forgot the gel he promised — the mild oath signals how unserious he takes this crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: What side of the road do you drive on?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: The middle.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: What language do you speak there?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: We pretty much just make it up as we go along.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's why the people from there are so hard to understand.

jokes.text: 'Hi, greg, I need some anti-glue.' — 'What's anti-glue, Murray?' — 'Well, it's anti-glue, isn't it? It gets rid of glue.' — 'I don't think that exists, Murray.' — 'Just get it, okay? It's an emergency.'
jokes.setup: Murray needs to deal with the glue crisis at the ceremony.
jokes.punchline: Murray invents 'anti-glue' as a product category and is confident it exists. Greg's 'I don't think that exists' is dismissed as incompetence.
jokes.explanation: Murray's absolute confidence in a product he has invented on the spot is perfectly Murray. His definition ('it's anti-glue, isn't it? It gets rid of glue') is circular and confident. The structure of Greg correctly identifying that this doesn't exist and being overridden anyway is a beautiful bureaucratic comedy beat. 'It's an emergency' as the clincher — treating anti-glue sourcing as urgent — is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It wasn't the gel that made you cool.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It wasn't?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It was the confidence the gel gave you.

jokes.text: Gary the sheep: 'The guy who was supposed to mime shear him sheared him for real. He's basically bald.' — 'Gary can tell. He's livid.' — 'And by the way, the guy next to him barbecuing... I think he's barbecuing lamb.'
jokes.setup: Gary the sheep (celebrity, famous for his look) was supposed to have mime-shearing to protect his appearance.
jokes.punchline: Gary has been accidentally fully shorn (bald), is aware of what happened and is angry about it, AND the person next to him appears to be barbecuing lamb, which Gary can presumably smell.
jokes.explanation: The episode's second-best joke and strongest callback payoff. The Gary setup — a sheep with a celebrity look requiring mime-shearing — pays off with three layers: (1) he's been actually shorn and is now bald, (2) Gary can tell this happened and is livid (the sheep has feelings and awareness), (3) someone is barbecuing lamb next to him. Each revelation escalates. 'Gary can tell. He's livid.' treats the sheep as a person with dignity and pride who has suffered a professional humiliation. The lamb barbecue is the dark cherry on top — threatening Gary with existential dread. Beautifully structured, long-setup callback.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was the hair gel, guys. Sorry. We're not cool? No.

jokes.text: Information booth: 'What side of the road do you drive on?' — 'The middle.' / 'What language do you speak there?' — 'We pretty much just make it up as we go along. That's why the people from there are so hard to understand.'
jokes.setup: Despite refusing the information booth job, Bret and Jemaine appear to be manning it, giving information about New Zealand.
jokes.punchline: Their answers are completely wrong/absurd. NZ drives on the left, not the middle; English is spoken there; and the 'we make it up' explanation for why New Zealanders are 'hard to understand' is surreal.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy from New Zealanders giving wrong information about New Zealand. 'The middle' for driving is wrong but logically odd-humored. But 'we pretty much just make it up as we go along' as the explanation for New Zealand English — and the self-deprecating acknowledgment that they're hard to understand — is very strong. This is FOTC at their best: self-aware, deadpan, and somehow both wrong and right about themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealandtown.

jokes.text: 'We can't do it. We're not cool enough.' — Murray: 'Guys, do you want to know a secret? It wasn't the gel that made you cool. It was the confidence the gel gave you.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine refuse to perform because without gel they feel uncool.
jokes.punchline: Murray delivers a sincere 'it was confidence all along' speech — which the episode then immediately refutes.
jokes.explanation: Murray's confidence speech functions as a setup for the gag that immediately follows — the show plays it straight enough that we might think they'll succeed, before revealing they can't. The speech itself is too earnest to be funny on its own, but it's structurally essential.

jokes.text: Murray's confidence speech: 'It wasn't the gel that made you cool. It was the confidence the gel gave you.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine feel they can't perform because they're not cool without gel.
jokes.punchline: Murray gives a sincere, almost moving speech about inner confidence — which would be touching — immediately followed by the revelation that no, actually it was the gel.
jokes.explanation: The speech itself is competent — it subverts the inspirational coach cliché by being specifically about hair gel. But it's only half the joke. The other half comes at minute 22.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, it was the hair gel, guys. Sorry. We're not cool? No.' / 'This is a new low, isn't it?' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Murray's confidence speech inspired them to perform. The performance is apparently a disaster.
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately retracts his inspirational speech and confirms it was the gel. 'This is a new low' returns — the episode's refrain, bookending the story.
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic button. Murray's inspirational speech is immediately retracted with 'Yeah, it was the hair gel, guys. Sorry.' — the most anti-climactic confirmation of failure imaginable. 'This is a new low' completing its arc as the episode's refrain (first used for one audience member, last used for the whole ceremony collapse) is structurally satisfying. Murray's honesty is both funny and characteristically honest in the wrong moment.

jokes.text: 'This was a complete disaster.' — 'Well, I thought that was relatively successful.' — 'Yes, it went very well. Well done, Paula.'
jokes.setup: New Zealandtown has apparently been a complete failure.
jokes.punchline: The PM and Murray independently assess it as successful while Bret calls it a complete disaster. No one reconciles these views.
jokes.explanation: The institutional obliviousness of Murray and the PM in contrast to Bret's accurate assessment is consistent character comedy. 'Well done, Paula' to the person who just watched a disaster unfold is a characteristically misplaced commendation.

jokes.text: The PM approaches Paula as she's leaving. 'Paula. Um, I...' — [23:19] 'nothing.' [23:23] 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: The PM has been clearly harboring feelings for Paula throughout the episode, stemming from their implied romantic history.
jokes.punchline: After building to a moment of confession, the PM says 'I...' — then 'nothing.' Paula says 'Okay.' and the moment dies. Nothing is resolved.
jokes.explanation: Masterful anti-comedy. The PM's 'nothing' after building to an emotional confession is devastating in its restraint, and Paula's 'Okay' — no follow-up, no pursuit — is perfect. The economy score is 10 because no other joke in the episode does as much with as little. The silence of [23:19] IS the joke. It's cringe comedy by omission, and the unresolved subtext of Murray and Paula and the PM forms the emotional texture underneath. FOTC excels at this kind of restrained, sad-funny beat.

jokes.text: 'Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealandtown.' — Final line of the episode.
jokes.setup: The PM failed to speak his feelings; Paula has left; New Zealandtown has failed.
jokes.punchline: A riff on 'Forget it, Jake. It's Chinatown.' — the famous Chinatown (1974) closing line, repurposed as a deflated New Zealand equivalent. The reference reframes the entire episode's New Zealandtown conceit as a small, failed, quietly sad version of a cinematic tragedy.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best single joke and a perfect closing line. 'Forget it, Jake. It's Chinatown.' is one of cinema's most famous closing lines — a fatalistic acknowledgment that corruption and loss are systemic. Here it becomes 'Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealandtown.' — the substitution of 'Bryan' and 'New Zealandtown' shrinks the tragedy to a deeply human, deeply minor scale. The joke works whether or not you know the reference (it still reads as a resignation) and perfectly if you do (the gap between Chinatown's weight and New Zealandtown's smallness is the joke). This is the kind of closing joke that makes an episode feel complete and memorable.

jokes.text: 'Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealand Town.' — closing line, riffing on 'Forget it, Jake, it's Chinatown.'
jokes.setup: The PM's 'nothing' moment with Paula, and the entire New Zealand Town venture having gone nowhere.
jokes.punchline: Murray closes the episode with a direct parody of the final line of Chinatown (1974) — 'Forget it, Jake, it's Chinatown' — with 'New Zealand Town' substituted, commenting simultaneously on the PM's romantic failure and the futility of the whole enterprise.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed closing joke. The Chinatown reference has been seeded throughout the episode (it's literally where New Zealand Town is placed). The substitution of 'New Zealand Town' for 'Chinatown' is funny on its face, but the joke earns its excellence through how perfectly it fits: the PM's failed romantic moment mirrors the tragic resignation of the original film's closing line, while simultaneously deflating the entire enterprise. Murray delivering this — the band's bumbling, low-expectations manager — with noir gravity is the final piece. The highest quotability score in the episode because it works on multiple levels and functions as the episode's perfect capstone. Justifies 8+ comfortably.

tmdb_overview: Trying to look cool for a gig at the Grand Opening of New York's one-block New Zealand enclave, the Conchords end up getting hooked on hair gel.
